title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Learning in Another Dimension: Virtual Reality Enters the Classroom
content.text: A class of twelve-year-olds in a school in Bristol is currently standing on the surface of Mars. They are examining rock formations, measuring craters and debating whether the reddish dust around them could ever support microbial life. Their teacher is standing at the back of the room watching them through a laptop screen. (37) [37] El aula física no ha cambiado, pero la experiencia de aprendizaje sí. [/37] None of the students has left the building; all of them are wearing virtual reality headsets. || VR technology in education is not entirely new. Early experiments in the 1990s produced bulky, expensive systems that schools could not afford and children could not use comfortably. (38) [38] Los primeros intentos fracasaron por razones técnicas y económicas. [/38] Today, lightweight headsets costing a few hundred pounds can deliver immersive experiences of striking quality, and a growing number of schools are finding ways to incorporate them into their lessons. || The potential benefits are considerable. Research suggests that immersive experiences improve retention: students who have virtually 'visited' a historical site or witnessed a scientific process tend to recall details more accurately than those who only read about them. (39) [39] La inmersión mejora la retención y la comprensión. [/39] For subjects such as history, geography, biology and physics, the technology offers experiences that are simply impossible to replicate in a conventional classroom. || There are, however, legitimate concerns. Extended use of VR headsets has been associated with eye strain and disorientation in some young users. (40) [40] La salud física de los estudiantes es una prioridad. [/40] Most educators recommend limiting individual VR sessions to fifteen or twenty minutes and ensuring that students have adequate breaks between uses. || The cost of equipping a full class remains a barrier for many schools, particularly in the state sector. (41) [41] La brecha económica amenaza con crear desigualdad educativa. [/41] Charities and government schemes are beginning to address this gap, providing shared headset libraries that multiple schools can borrow from on a rota. || Looking ahead, developers are working on platforms that allow students in different locations to share the same virtual space simultaneously. (42) [42] La colaboración virtual entre escuelas podría transformar la educación global. [/42] A student in Lagos and a classmate in London could one day explore an ancient Egyptian tomb side by side, their avatars moving through the same digital world in real time.

paragraphs.A: Educators also point out that this kind of embodied learning tends to spark curiosity and motivate further independent research.
paragraphs.B: Guidelines on screen time and physical wellbeing must therefore be built into any responsible VR programme.
paragraphs.C: This disparity risks creating a two-tier education system in which only well-funded schools can offer immersive learning.
paragraphs.D: Such collaborative virtual environments could make distance learning far more engaging than video calls alone.
paragraphs.E: Those early systems suffered from poor resolution, motion sickness and prohibitive costs that put them beyond any school budget.
paragraphs.F: The physical classroom may look the same from the outside, but the learning experience inside has been fundamentally altered.
paragraphs.G: Pilot studies have reported significant gains in test scores among students who used VR as part of their revision.

questions.explanation: F connects the description of the outdoor classroom with the transformed inner experience before mentioning the virtual reality headsets.
E explains the technical and economic problems of older systems, describing why they failed.
A complements the improvement in retention with the motivation that curiosity generates, before discussing the benefits by subject.
B introduces the need for physical wellbeing guidelines in response to concerns about prolonged use.
C points out the educational inequality that the economic gap can generate before mentioning shared lending solutions.
D describes the potential of collaborative virtual environments before the specific example of Lagos and London.
